**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three advantages of antifuse-based FPGAs over SRAM-based FPGAs.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Non-volatility:** Configuration is retained without power; no external boot PROM is required. (1 mark)
2. **Radiation hardness:** Antifuses are immune to single-event upsets (SEU) because the programmed state is a physical, not a charge-based, phenomenon. (1 mark)
3. **Security:** The programmed routing is extremely difficult to reverse-engineer because antifuses cannot be electrically "read back" like a SRAM configuration memory. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Internal Choice Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Module 2 carries ~14 marks; students choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a PLICE antifuse used in FPGAs. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the programming voltage required for an antifuse with an ONO thickness of 12 nm, given the dielectric breakdown field of the oxide layer is 9 MV/cm. Comment on the design margin used in practice. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
The PLICE (Programmable Low-Impedance Circuit Element) antifuse consists of three layers sandwiched between two metal interconnect lines of the FPGA:

1. A **bottom electrode** formed by an $n+$ diffusion inside an $n$-well (connected to Metal-2).
2. An **ONO dielectric** (Oxide-Nitride-Oxide), approximately **10 nm thick**, acting as the insulating barrier.
3. A **top electrode** made of $n+$ polysilicon (connected to Metal-1).

**Working (3.5 marks):**
- In the **virgin (unprogrammed) state**, no current flows between the metal lines because the ONO dielectric is intact — the antifuse behaves as an open switch with $R_{off} > 1\ \text{G}\Omega$.
- During programming, a high voltage $V_{pgm} = 12\text{–}20\ \text{V}$ is applied across the electrodes. The induced electric field ($\sim 10^{9}\ \text{V/m}$) exceeds the **dielectric breakdown strength** of the ONO layer.
- **Avalanche / Fowler-Nordheim tunneling** breaks down the dielectric, creating a conductive filament of poly-Si and trapped metal ions bridging the electrodes.
- After programming, $R_{on} \approx 20\text{–}100\ \Omega$, and the link is **permanent** (no charge storage, so it cannot decay).
- Normal circuit operation at $V_{cc} = 1.8\ \text{V}$ produces fields far below $\mathcal{E}_{bd}$, so the link remains stable for the device's lifetime.

**Diagram (1 mark included in 3.5 above):** A labelled cross-section (Metal-1 → Poly → ONO → n+ → n-well → Metal-2) earns the diagram credit.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given:** $t_{ox} = 12\ \text{nm} = 12 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m} = 1.2 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}$, $\mathcal{E}_{bd} = 9\ \text{MV/cm} = 9 \times 10^{8}\ \text{V/m}$.

**Step 1 — Programming field relation.**

$$
V_{pgm} = \mathcal{E}_{bd} \cdot t_{ox}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and compute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{pgm} &= (9 \times 10^{8}\ \text{V/m}) \times (1.2 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}) \\
&= 10.8\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Design margin comment (3 marks for the full derivation + comment):**
The theoretical minimum is **10.8 V**, but in practice, designers add a **safety margin of 20–50 %** to account for:
- Process variations in ONO thickness.
- Contamination and surface defects in the dielectric.
- Partial breakdowns requiring multiple pulses.

Hence, a typical applied programming voltage is **13 V to 16 V**. This voltage is generated on-chip by a **charge pump circuit** (e.g., Dickson charge pump) using the external $V_{cc}$ as the source.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare antifuse programming technology with SRAM-based programming technology in FPGAs. Discuss the suitability of each for **(i)** aerospace applications and **(ii)** prototyping. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An antifuse in a routing channel of a Microsemi RTAX FPGA has an unprogrammed resistance of 1.2 GΩ and a programmed resistance of 40 Ω. A signal net must be routed through 6 antifuses in series. Compute the equivalent resistance in the **(i)** unprogrammed (open) state and **(ii)** fully programmed (closed) state. Comment on the design implication. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Comparison table (4 marks):**

| Feature | **Antifuse** | **SRAM** |
|---|---|---|
| Storage mechanism | Permanent dielectric breakdown | Volatile latch (6T cell) |
| Volatility | **Non-volatile** | **Volatile** (reloads on power-up) |
| Programmability | OTP | In-system, infinite |
| Area per bit | Small | Large (6 transistors) |
| Security | **High** (no readout) | Low (bitstream interceptable) |
| Radiation tolerance | **Excellent** (rad-hard) | Poor (SEU in flip-flops & CRAM) |

**Suitability discussion (3 marks):**

- **(i) Aerospace applications:** Antifuse FPGAs are **preferred** because of their **radiation hardness** (no SEU), **instant power-on** (no boot), and **high security** (tamper-resistant). SRAM FPGAs require TMR (triple modular redundancy) and scrubbing to be used in space, adding area, power, and complexity.
- **(ii) Prototyping:** SRAM FPGAs are **preferred** because they support **in-system, unlimited reprogramming** via JTAG, allowing fast design iteration. Antifuse FPGAs cannot be re-used — a design bug requires a new chip, making them uneconomical for prototyping.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given:** $R_{off} = 1.2\ \text{G}\Omega = 1.2 \times 10^{9}\ \Omega$, $R_{on} = 40\ \Omega$, $n = 6$ antifuses in series.

**Step 1 — Unprogrammed (open) state: resistors in series add up.**

$$
R_{eq,off} = n \cdot R_{off} = 6 \times 1.2\ \text{G}\Omega = 7.2\ \text{G}\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Fully programmed (closed) state.**

$$
R_{eq,on} = n \cdot R_{on} = 6 \times 40\ \Omega = 240\ \Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Design implication (3 marks).**

The on/off ratio of the programmed net is:

$$
\rho_{net} = \frac{R_{eq,off}}{R_{eq,on}} = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{9}}{240} = 3 \times 10^{7}
$$

Even with 6 antifuses in series, the **on/off resistance ratio remains > 10⁷**, ensuring excellent signal isolation. However, the **240 Ω** series resistance is significant: it consumes voltage headroom and slows RC edges. Designers therefore **minimize the number of series antifuses in a timing-critical path**, often using dedicated **fast-path** or **direct-connect** routing channels that bypass the antifuse matrix.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "antifuse" with "poly fuse"** (a current-blown metal link) — antifuses are **voltage-programmed**, not current-blown.
> 2. **Common mark loss:** Students often write "antifuse stores a charge" — this is **wrong**; antifuses store a **physical conductive filament** and are therefore immune to charge leakage.
> 3. **Units trap:** When computing $V_{pgm} = \mathcal{E}_{bd} \cdot t_{ox}$, **always convert nm → m and MV/cm → V/m** before multiplying. Mixing units is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Antifuse area cost:** Antifuses are *area-efficient per bit*, but routing in antifuse FPGAs is *less flexible* than SRAM FPGAs (no run-time reconfiguration). Do **not** claim antifuses offer the "same flexibility" — they do not.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Antifuse = open → closed** (logical complement of a fuse).
- **Unprogrammed state:** $R_{off} > 1\ \text{G}\Omega$ (open switch).
- **Programmed state:** $R_{on} \approx 20\text{–}100\ \Omega$ (closed switch, permanent).
- **Programming mechanism:** ONO dielectric breakdown at $V_{pgm} = 12\text{–}20\ \text{V}$ for $\sim 100\ \mu\text{s}$.
- **Programming energy:** $\sim 7.5\ \mu\text{J}$ per antifuse (for $V=15$ V, $I=5$ mA, $t=100\ \mu$s).
- **PLICE structure:** Metal-1 → $n+$ Polysilicon → ONO (10 nm) → $n+$ Diffusion in n-well → Metal-2.
- **Programming field:** $\mathcal{E}_{pgm} = V_{pgm}/t_{ox} \geq 10\ \text{MV/cm}$.
- **Non-volatile & OTP** — once programmed, **cannot be changed**.
- **Radiation hard** — no charge storage → immune to SEU.
- **High security** — no read-back possible; used in defense/aerospace.
- **No boot PROM required** — instant power-on configuration.
- **Drawbacks:** (1) **One-time programmable** (no design iteration), (2) needs **on-chip charge pump** for $V_{pgm}$, (3) **higher series resistance** in long routing paths.
- **Vendors:** Microsemi/Microchip (ACT1, ACT2, ACT3, Axcelerator, **RTAX-S**, **RTAX-DSP**).
- **Use $V_{pgm} = \mathcal{E}_{bd} \cdot t_{ox}$** with units in **SI** to compute programming voltage.
- **Resistance ratio** $R_{off}/R_{on} \geq 10^7$ gives excellent noise margin in PIPs.
- **Antifuse is the only FPGA programming element that is BOTH non-volatile AND immune to radiation** in a single cell.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SRAM and EPROM Based FPGAs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT502 - FPGA BASED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SRAM and EPROM Based FPGAs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 SRAM-Based FPGAs

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
A **Static RAM (SRAM)-based FPGA** is a reprogrammable logic device in which the configuration memory (which programs the logic blocks, interconnect, and I/O) is implemented using **Static Random Access Memory cells**. The configuration data is stored in distributed SRAM cells distributed across the entire chip. Since SRAM is **volatile**, the configuration bitstream must be reloaded from an external non-volatile memory (PROM, Flash, or microcontroller) every time the device is powered up.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** SRAM-based FPGAs are **reprogrammable an unlimited number of times in-system** and are the dominant architecture used by industry leaders like **Xilinx (Spartan, Virtex, Artix, Kintex, Ultrascale series)** and **Intel/Altera (Cyclone, Stratix series)**.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Whiteboard vs. The Carved Stone"**
Think of an SRAM-based FPGA like a **whiteboard** in a conference room. Every morning, before any meeting, someone must walk in and re-write the previous day's diagrams on the board (this is the **configuration reload** at power-up). If the power goes out, the board is wiped clean, but the master copy of the diagrams is safely stored in a filing cabinet (the external non-volatile memory). The advantage is that you can erase and re-draw new designs **as many times as you want** without any wear and tear.

### 1.2 EPROM-Based FPGAs

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
An **EPROM-based FPGA** uses **Erasable Programmable Read-Only Memory** transistors as the configuration memory element. The configuration is stored by **injecting hot electrons (Fowler-Nordheim tunneling or avalanche injection)** into the floating gate of a MOSFET. The stored charge permanently alters the threshold voltage of the transistor, retaining the configuration **even when power is removed** (non-volatile). Erasure is performed by **exposing the silicon die to ultraviolet (UV) light** for 20–30 minutes, which photoelectrically discharges the floating gates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** EPROM-based FPGAs are **non-volatile but not in-system programmable**. They require removal from the circuit board and a UV eraser to be reprogrammed, which made them obsolete for most modern applications but historically important in devices like the **Xilinx XC2000, XC3000, and Altera Classic / MAX 5000 series**.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Carved Stone Tablet"**
An EPROM-based FPGA is like a **carved stone tablet**. Once carved (programmed), the design stays forever, even if you take the tablet home and turn off all the lights. To change the design, you must place the stone in a special UV-light "sandbox" for 30 minutes to "melt" the carvings, and then re-carve it using a special programming tool.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics for Configuration Memory

| Parameter | SRAM Cell | EPROM Cell |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Volatility** | Volatile | Non-Volatile |
| **Cell Size** | **6 transistors** (6T) | **1 transistor** (1T floating gate) |
| **Reprogrammability** | In-system, infinite cycles | Out-of-system, limited (UV erase) |
| **Technology Node** | Standard CMOS | Standard CMOS + floating gate |
| **Power-on Loading** | Required (from external ROM) | Not required (self-contained) |
| **Typical Usage Era** | 1985 – Present | 1984 – 1995 (legacy) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner Tip:** When asked to compare the two, the examiner expects students to state **three points minimum**: (1) volatility, (2) reprogrammability, and (3) cell size / transistor count. Memorize the **6T SRAM cell** count — this is a frequently-asked question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The SRAM Configuration Cell — How It Stores a Bit

The configuration memory of an SRAM-based FPGA is built from a classic **6-transistor (6T) SRAM cell**, identical to the cache memory in a CPU. The cell consists of:

1. **Two cross-coupled inverters (4 transistors: M1, M2, M3, M4)** — these form a bistable latch that holds one of two stable states (logic '0' or logic '1').
2. **Two access/pass transistors (M5, M6)** — these are used to read from or write into the cell when the word line (WL) is activated.

**Operational Steps of a 6T SRAM Cell:**
- **Hold Mode:** WL = 0, pass transistors OFF. The cross-coupled inverters maintain the stored bit indefinitely as long as V_DD is supplied.
- **Read Mode:** WL = 1, precharge the bit lines (BL and ~BL), then sense the differential voltage.
- **Write Mode:** WL = 1, drive BL and ~BL with the new data; the strong write drivers overpower the inverters, flipping the latch.

**The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
- **Why 6T and not 4T?** A 4T cell uses a resistive load which is unstable and leaky. The 6T cell is **fully static** and has near-zero static power, which is critical because a modern FPGA may contain **tens of millions** of these cells.
- **Why cross-coupled inverters?** They create **positive feedback**, ensuring the cell is noise-immune. Any attempt to flip the state is reinforced by the inverters themselves.

### 2.2 The EPROM Configuration Cell — Hot-Electron Injection

The EPROM cell uses a **floating-gate avalanche-injection MOS (FAMOS)** transistor:
- A standard MOSFET has a control gate on top of a thin gate oxide.
- The EPROM transistor adds a **second, completely isolated "floating gate"** sandwiched between the control gate and the channel.
- During programming, a **high drain-to-source voltage (V_DS ≈ 12V to 20V)** causes **avalanche breakdown** near the drain. Hot electrons gain enough kinetic energy to "jump" through the thin oxide and get trapped on the floating gate.
- Once trapped, these electrons remain there for **decades** (retention > 10 years) because the floating gate is surrounded by insulating SiO₂.

**The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
- **Why high voltage?** The SiO₂ barrier is ~3.2 eV. Electrons at normal V_DD (3.3V or 1.8V) cannot possibly clear this barrier. The high voltage accelerates them to high energy (hot carriers).
- **Why UV light for erasure?** UV photons at 253.7 nm wavelength have enough energy to **photo-excite** the trapped electrons, allowing them to tunnel back out of the floating gate into the silicon substrate.

### 2.3 Configuration Architecture in an FPGA

In an SRAM FPGA, the configuration bitstream does not only program the logic — it programs **everything**:
- **Look-Up Table (LUT) contents** — defining the Boolean function of each logic block.
- **Multiplexer select lines** — routing signals through the interconnect network.
- **Switch matrices** — closing/opening pass-transistor switches that form the routing paths.
- **I/O standard selection** — choosing LVCMOS, LVDS, SSTL, etc.
- **Internal registers' initial values** — defining power-on state of flip-flops.
- **Block RAM initialization** — preloading memory contents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A single Xilinx 7-series FPGA may require **tens of megabits** of configuration data. For example, the XC7A100T requires ~26 Mbit of configuration bitstream. This is why the external boot PROM must be carefully sized.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Typical Value | Unit | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Config. Memory Size** | $M_{cfg} = N_{LUT} \times 2^{k} + M_{BRAM} + M_{IO} + M_{switch}$ | 1 – 100 | Mbits | Total bitstream size for the FPGA |
| **Config. Time (Serial)** | $t_{cfg} = \dfrac{M_{cfg}}{f_{clk} \times w_{bus}}$ | 10 – 500 | ms | Time to load the FPGA at power-up |
| **SRAM Cell Transistor Count** | $N_{T,SRAM} = 6$ | 6 | Transistors | Determines silicon area per config. bit |
| **EPROM Cell Transistor Count** | $N_{T,EPROM} = 1$ | 1 | Transistor | Much denser configuration memory |
| **Config. Clock Frequency** | $f_{CCLK}$ | 2 – 100 | MHz | Serial configuration clock on the FPGA |
| **Bitstream Width (SelectMAP)** | $w_{bus}$ | 8 / 16 / 32 | bits | Parallel configuration bus width |
| **Programming Voltage (EPROM)** | $V_{PP}$ | 12 – 21 | V | Required to inject hot electrons |
| **UV Erase Time** | $t_{erase}$ | 20 – 30 | minutes | Time to discharge floating gates |
| **Data Retention (EPROM)** | $t_{retention}$ | $\geq 10$ | years | Charge retention on floating gate |
| **Endurance (SRAM)** | $N_{cycles}$ | $\infty$ (unlimited) | cycles | SRAM has no write wear-out |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- **SRAM FPGAs** dominate **prototyping, ASIC emulation, DSP, aerospace re-configurable computing, and 5G baseband processing** because of their infinite reprogrammability.
- **EPROM FPGAs** (legacy) are still found in **vintage aerospace and military systems** where in-field updates are not required and non-volatility is critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Configuration Time of an SRAM FPGA

The total time required to configure an SRAM-based FPGA at power-up is a function of the bitstream size, the configuration clock frequency, and the data bus width. We will derive the closed-form expression step by step.

**Given:**
- Total configuration bitstream size $= M_{cfg}$ (in bits)
- Configuration clock frequency $= f_{CCLK}$ (in Hz)
- Data bus width (parallel words per clock) $= w_{bus}$ (in bits)

**Step 1 — Bits loaded per clock cycle:**
In one clock period $T_{clk} = 1 / f_{CCLK}$, the FPGA receives $w_{bus}$ bits in parallel (or 1 bit for serial mode).

$$
b_{per\_cycle} = w_{bus} \quad \text{[bits per clock]}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of clock cycles required:**
The total number of clock cycles needed to shift the entire bitstream is the total bits divided by the bits-per-cycle.

$$
N_{cycles} = \frac{M_{cfg}}{b_{per\_cycle}} = \frac{M_{cfg}}{w_{bus}}
$$

**Step 3 — Total configuration time:**
The total time is the number of cycles multiplied by the clock period.

$$
t_{cfg} = N_{cycles} \times T_{clk} = \frac{M_{cfg}}{w_{bus}} \times \frac{1}{f_{CCLK}}
$$

Therefore, the **final expression** is:

$$
t_{cfg} = \frac{M_{cfg}}{f_{CCLK} \times w_{bus}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style):**
An SRAM FPGA requires $M_{cfg} = 16$ Mbits of configuration data. The serial configuration clock is $f_{CCLK} = 50$ MHz with $w_{bus} = 1$ bit (serial mode).

$$
t_{cfg} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{50 \times 10^{6} \times 1} = 0.32 \text{ seconds}
$$

For a parallel SelectMAP configuration with $w_{bus} = 8$ bits:

$$
t_{cfg} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{50 \times 10^{6} \times 8} = 0.04 \text{ seconds} = 40 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of the conversion:** We converted 16 Mbits to $16 \times 10^6$ bits and 50 MHz to $50 \times 10^6$ Hz, then performed straight division since the units $\frac{\text{bits}}{\text{bits/sec}} = \text{seconds}$ cancel out dimensionally.

### 3.2 Derivation: Density Advantage of EPROM over SRAM

**Given:** Both SRAM (6T) and EPROM (1T) cells are fabricated on the same CMOS process node with minimum feature size $F$ (in nm).

**Step 1 — Approximate silicon area per cell:**
- A 6T SRAM cell occupies roughly $120 F^2$ of silicon area.
- A 1T EPROM cell occupies roughly $20 F^2$ of silicon area.

**Step 2 — Cell density ratio (bits per unit area):**
The density is inversely proportional to the cell area.

$$
\rho_{SRAM} = \frac{1}{120 F^2} \quad \text{and} \quad \rho_{EPROM} = \frac{1}{20 F^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Density advantage ratio:**

$$
R_{density} = \frac{\rho_{EPROM}}{\rho_{SRAM}} = \frac{1 / 20 F^2}{1 / 120 F^2} = \frac{120}{20} = 6
$$

Therefore, the **EPROM cell is 6× denser** than the SRAM cell, meaning the same silicon area can hold 6 times more configuration bits in an EPROM-based FPGA.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why don't modern FPGAs use EPROM?** Although EPROM is denser and non-volatile, its **inability to be reprogrammed in-system** and the **high programming voltage** (12–21V) made it incompatible with modern low-voltage, in-system-reconfigurable designs. The flexibility of SRAM won the market.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | SRAM-Based FPGA | EPROM-Based FPGA | Winner / Verdict |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reprogrammability** | In-system, infinite | Out-of-system, UV erase | **SRAM** |
| **Non-Volatility** | Volatile (needs boot PROM) | Non-volatile | **EPROM** |
| **Cell Density** | $120 F^2$ (6T) | $20 F^2$ (1T) | **EPROM (6× denser)** |
| **Power-on Time** | Slow (must load bitstream) | Fast (instant on) | **EPROM** |
| **Static Power** | Low (pure CMOS) | Very low (no leakage from floating gate) | **Tie** |
| **Technology Compatibility** | Standard CMOS | Requires special floating-gate process | **SRAM (easier)** |
| **Endurance** | Unlimited write cycles | Limited (~1000 erase cycles) | **SRAM** |
| **Modern Usage** | Industry standard (Xilinx, Intel) | Obsolete (legacy only) | **SRAM** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — SRAM-Based FPGA Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the major architectural blocks of an SRAM-based FPGA and the role of the configuration SRAM in programming each block.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXT["External Non-Volatile Memory"]
        PROM1["Configuration PROM / Flash"]
    end
    subgraph FPGA["SRAM-Based FPGA Chip"]
        CFG_CTRL["Configuration Controller"]
        SRAM_ARR["Distributed SRAM Config. Memory"]
        LUT_BLK["Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs / LABs) with LUTs and FFs"]
        SW_MATRIX["Programmable Switch Matrix"]
        IOB_BLK["I/O Blocks (IOBs)"]
        BRAM_BLK["Block RAM (BRAM)"]
        DSP_BLK["DSP / Multiply-Accumulate Blocks"]
        CLK_BLK["Clock Management Tiles (PLL / MMCM)"]
    end
    PROM1 -- "Serial/Parallel Bitstream at Power-On" --> CFG_CTRL
    CFG_CTRL -- "Writes config bits row by row" --> SRAM_ARR
    SRAM_ARR -- "Programs LUT contents" --> LUT_BLK
    SRAM_ARR -- "Programs MUX selects for routing" --> SW_MATRIX
    SRAM_ARR -- "Programs I/O standard and drive strength" --> IOB_BLK
    SRAM_ARR -- "Programs BRAM init data" --> BRAM_BLK
    SRAM_ARR -- "Programs DSP mode" --> DSP_BLK
    SRAM_ARR -- "Programs PLL frequency and phase" --> CLK_BLK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** At power-on, the bitstream flows from the external PROM into the configuration controller, which writes the data into the distributed SRAM cells. These SRAM cells then drive the select lines and control inputs of every programmable element on the chip simultaneously.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — 6T SRAM Configuration Cell

The following diagram shows the transistor-level structure of the 6T SRAM cell used in FPGA configuration memory.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BL["BL (Bit Line)"] --- M5["M5 (Access)"]
    BLB["~BL (Bit Line Bar)"] --- M6["M6 (Access)"]
    WL["WL (Word Line)"] --- G5["G of M5"]
    WL --- G6["G of M6"]
    M5 --- Q["Q (Storage Node)"]
    M6 --- QB["~Q (Storage Node Bar)"]
    M1["M1 (Pull-up PMOS)"] --- VDD["V_DD"]
    M1 --- Q
    M3["M3 (Pull-down NMOS)"] --- Q
    M3 --- GND1["GND"]
    M2["M2 (Pull-up PMOS)"] --- VDD
    M2 --- QB
    M4["M4 (Pull-down NMOS)"] --- QB
    M4 --- GND2["GND"]
    QB --- G1["G of M1 / M3"]
    Q --- G2["G of M2 / M4"]
```

**Description of operation:**
- The cross-coupled pair (M1–M2 as PMOS pull-ups, M3–M4 as NMOS pull-downs) forms two inverters whose outputs feed back to each other's inputs.
- M5 and M6 are NMOS access transistors gated by the word line (WL).
- When WL = 0, the cell is isolated and holds its state (hold mode).
- When WL = 1, the cell can be read (via BL/~BL sensing) or written (by forcing BL/~BL).

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — EPROM Programming and UV Erasure Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROG["Programming Phase (V_PP = 12 to 21 V)"]
        VPP1["High V_DS Applied"] --> AVA["Avalanche Breakdown near Drain"]
        AVA --> HEI["Hot Electrons Generated"]
        HEI --> INJ["Electrons Tunnel through Thin Gate Oxide"]
        INJ --> FG1["Negative Charge Trapped on Floating Gate"]
    end
    subgraph ERASE["Erasure Phase (UV Light 253.7 nm)"]
        UVL["UV Lamp Exposure for 20 to 30 min"] --> PHOTO["Photons Excite Trapped Electrons"]
        PHOTO --> TUNNEL2["Electrons Tunnel Back to Substrate"]
        TUNNEL2 --> FG2["Floating Gate Discharged"]
    end
    FG1 --> AFTER["Programmed Cell (Threshold V_T shifted high)"]
    FG2 --> AFTER2["Erased Cell (Restored to original V_T)"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the threshold voltage changes:** Adding negative charge to the floating gate makes it harder to turn on the transistor. A programmed EPROM transistor has a high threshold voltage (effectively OFF), while an erased one has a normal low threshold (effectively ON). The FPGA senses this V_T difference to determine whether the configuration bit is '0' or '1'.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**Q:** What is meant by SRAM-based FPGA? Why is an external non-volatile memory required for its operation?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*(1 mark)* An SRAM-based FPGA is a programmable logic device in which the configuration memory (used to program logic, interconnect, and I/O) is implemented using Static RAM cells distributed across the chip.
*(1 mark)* Since SRAM is **volatile**, the configuration data is lost when power is removed.
*(1 mark)* Therefore, an external non-volatile memory such as a configuration PROM, Flash memory, or a microcontroller is required to store the bitstream and reload it into the FPGA at every power-up, enabling the FPGA to function correctly.

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2022]**
**Q:** List any three differences between SRAM-based and EPROM-based FPGAs.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark Each):**
1. **Volatility:** SRAM-based FPGAs are **volatile** and need reconfiguration at every power-on, whereas EPROM-based FPGAs are **non-volatile** and retain their configuration after power-off.
2. **Reprogrammability:** SRAM-based FPGAs can be **reprogrammed in-system an unlimited number of times**, whereas EPROM-based FPGAs require removal from the board and **UV-light erasure (20–30 minutes)** for reprogramming.
3. **Cell Structure:** The SRAM configuration cell uses **6 transistors (6T)** with two cross-coupled inverters, whereas the EPROM cell uses a **single floating-gate transistor (1T FAMOS)**, making EPROM approximately 6× denser.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**Q (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an SRAM-based FPGA. List the major programmable elements and explain the role of the configuration controller. (7 Marks)
**Q (b)** Derive the expression for configuration loading time of an SRAM-based FPGA. An FPGA requires 32 Mbit of configuration data and uses a serial configuration clock of 100 MHz. Calculate the configuration time. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks Architecture Explanation:**
*(1 mark)* The SRAM-based FPGA consists of an array of **Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs)** for combinational and sequential logic, surrounded by a **programmable interconnect network** of switch matrices and routing channels, with **I/O Blocks (IOBs)** along the periphery.
*(1 mark)* Modern FPGAs also include dedicated **Block RAM (BRAM)** for on-chip memory, **DSP slices** for arithmetic, and **Clock Management Tiles (PLL/MMCM)** for clock conditioning.
*(1 mark)* A **distributed SRAM configuration memory** is spread throughout the chip, with separate banks for the logic, routing, I/O, and BRAM blocks. Each SRAM cell drives one configuration bit (e.g., a LUT bit, a MUX select line, or an I/O standard select).
*(1 mark)* The **Configuration Controller** is a dedicated state machine that interfaces with the external boot PROM. At power-on, it reads the bitstream serially (or in parallel) from the PROM and writes it row-by-row into the configuration SRAM.
*(1 mark)* The controller also handles **multi-frame write, CRC checking, readback, and partial reconfiguration** in advanced devices.
*(2 marks)* For diagram, refer to Section 4.1 of these notes. A well-labeled block diagram earns full credit.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks Derivation + Numerical:**
*(2 marks)* The configuration time formula is derived as:
$$t_{cfg} = N_{cycles} \times T_{clk}$$
*(1 mark)* Substituting $N_{cycles} = \dfrac{M_{cfg}}{w_{bus}}$ and $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{CCLK}}$:
$$t_{cfg} = \frac{M_{cfg}}{f_{CCLK} \times w_{bus}}$$
*(1 mark)* Given: $M_{cfg} = 32 \text{ Mbit} = 32 \times 10^6 \text{ bits}$, $f_{CCLK} = 100 \text{ MHz} = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$, $w_{bus} = 1$ (serial).
*(1 mark)* Calculation:
$$t_{cfg} = \frac{32 \times 10^{6}}{100 \times 10^{6} \times 1} = 0.32 \text{ seconds} = 320 \text{ ms}$$
*(1 mark)* Unit verification: $\frac{\text{bits}}{\text{Hz} \times \text{bits}} = \frac{\text{bits}}{\text{bits/sec}} = \text{seconds}$ ✓
*(1 mark)* Real-world interpretation: 320 ms is the boot-up delay the system experiences at every power-on. Parallel SelectMAP mode ($w_{bus} = 8$) would reduce this to 40 ms.

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper 1]**
**Q (a)** Explain the structure and operation of a 6T SRAM cell used as a configuration memory element in FPGAs. (7 Marks)
**Q (b)** Explain the programming and erasure mechanism of an EPROM cell used in EPROM-based FPGAs. Compare the density of EPROM and SRAM configuration cells. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks 6T SRAM Cell:**
*(2 marks)* The 6T SRAM cell consists of **6 MOSFETs**: two PMOS pull-up transistors (M1, M2), two NMOS pull-down transistors (M3, M4), and two NMOS access transistors (M5, M6). M1–M4 form two cross-coupled CMOS inverters, while M5–M6 connect the internal storage nodes (Q and ~Q) to the bit lines (BL and ~BL) under the control of the word line (WL).
*(1 mark)* **Hold Mode:** WL = 0, access transistors OFF, the cross-coupled inverters maintain the stored bit indefinitely (static, no refresh required).
*(1 mark)* **Read Mode:** WL = 1, BL and ~BL are precharged high, then the cell pulls one of them low depending on the stored state, and a sense amplifier detects the differential.
*(1 mark)* **Write Mode:** WL = 1, BL and ~BL are driven strongly with the new data; the write drivers overpower the inverters, flipping the latch.
*(1 mark)* **Why 6T is preferred:** Fully static (no refresh), high noise immunity, near-zero static power dissipation — critical because an FPGA contains millions of these cells.
*(1 mark)* Diagram of the 6T cell earns full credit (refer to Section 4.2 of these notes).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks EPROM Programming and Comparison:**
*(1 mark)* The EPROM cell uses a **FAMOS (Floating-gate Avalanche-injection MOS)** transistor with a second "floating gate" electrically isolated inside the gate oxide.
*(1 mark)* **Programming:** A high drain-to-source voltage ($V_{PP}$ = 12 to 21 V) causes **avalanche breakdown** near the drain, generating hot electrons that tunnel through the thin oxide and accumulate on the floating gate. The trapped negative charge raises the threshold voltage ($V_T$) of the transistor, effectively turning it OFF.
*(1 mark)* **Erasure:** Exposure to **UV light at 253.7 nm** for 20–30 minutes photo-excites the trapped electrons, allowing them to tunnel back to the substrate. The $V_T$ returns to its normal value.
*(1 mark)* **Density Comparison:** SRAM cell area $\approx 120 F^2$ (6T); EPROM cell area $\approx 20 F^2$ (1T). Ratio = $120 / 20 = 6$, so EPROM is **6× denser** than SRAM.
*(2 marks)* **Trade-off Verdict:** Although EPROM is denser and non-volatile, it lost the market to SRAM due to: (i) inability to be reprogrammed in-system, (ii) high programming voltage requirement, (iii) limited erase endurance (~1000 cycles), and (iv) the need for a quartz window package. Hence, **SRAM-based FPGAs dominate the modern industry**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students write "SRAM is non-volatile" or "EPROM is volatile" — this is the **#1 reason for losing 1 mark** in Part A. SRAM is **always volatile**; EPROM/EEPROM/Flash is **always non-volatile**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** In derivation questions, students forget to **state the units** after the final numerical answer. The examiner will deduct 0.5 mark if the unit is missing.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** In Part B block-diagram questions, students draw a generic "FPGA" box without labeling the **configuration controller, configuration SRAM, CLB, and switch matrix** as separate blocks. Each missing label costs 0.5–1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing the **6T count** (SRAM cell) with the **1T count** (EPROM cell). The cell transistor count is the most-asked numerical fact in this module.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **SRAM-based FPGA** = **volatile**, **in-system reprogrammable**, **6T cell**, dominant modern architecture.
- ⭐ **EPROM-based FPGA** = **non-volatile**, **UV-erasable only**, **1T floating-gate cell**, legacy architecture.
- ⭐ **SRAM cell count = 6 transistors** (4 for cross-coupled inverters + 2 for access).
- ⭐ **EPROM cell count = 1 transistor** (FAMOS floating-gate structure).
- ⭐ **EPROM is ~6× denser** than SRAM in configuration memory.
- ⭐ **Programming voltage for EPROM = 12 to 21 V**; **UV erase time = 20 to 30 minutes**.
- ⭐ **Data retention of EPROM = ≥ 10 years**; **Endurance of SRAM = unlimited cycles**.
- ⭐ **Configuration loading time formula:** $t_{cfg} = \dfrac{M_{cfg}}{f_{CCLK} \times w_{bus}}$
- ⭐ **Configuration flow:** External PROM → Configuration Controller → Distributed SRAM → Programs CLB / Switch Matrix / IOB / BRAM / DSP / PLL.
- ⭐ **Industry leaders in SRAM FPGAs:** Xilinx (Spartan, Virtex, 7-series, Ultrascale+, Versal) and Intel/Altera (Cyclone, Stratix, Agilex).
- ⭐ **Legacy EPROM FPGA examples:** Xilinx XC2000, XC3000, XC4000 (early) and Altera Classic, MAX 5000.
- ⭐ **Always mention volatility, reprogrammability, and cell structure** when comparing — these are the three mandatory points.
- ⭐ **Always carry the units** in numerical answers: bits, Hz, seconds.
- ⭐ **Block diagrams must include** the configuration controller, the distributed configuration SRAM, and the major programmable blocks (CLB, switch matrix, IOB).
- ⭐ **Hot-electron injection** (avalanche) is the physical mechanism for EPROM programming; **Fowler-Nordheim tunneling** is the physical mechanism for EEPROM/Flash programming — do not confuse the two.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FPGA Logic Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT502 - FPGA BASED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** FPGA Logic Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **FPGA Logic Cell** (also referred to as a **Logic Element (LE)** in Altera/Intel terminology, or a **Basic Logic Element** within a CLB slice in Xilinx terminology) is the fundamental, fine-grained, reconfigurable computational primitive that constitutes the core fabric of a Field-Programmable Gate Array. It is the smallest unit of logic that can be independently programmed to implement a combinational or sequential boolean function.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the logic cell is defined as a tightly integrated cluster of four primary sub-circuits:
1. A **k-input Look-Up Table (LUT)** that synthesizes arbitrary combinational logic.
2. A **programmable D-type Flip-Flop (DFF)** that provides sequential (clocked) state retention.
3. A **fast carry-chain arithmetic logic** (Full Adder + Carry Propagate/Generate MUX) for high-speed arithmetic.
4. A **local routing multiplexer network** that selects between registered and combinational outputs and routes signals to neighboring cells.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Logic Cell" in modern FPGAs is more accurately a **macro** composed of multiple sub-elements. Modern Altera devices (Cyclone, Stratix) call this the *Logic Element (LE)*, while Xilinx (Spartan, Virtex, Artix, Kintex) calls it a *Slice* (containing multiple *LUTs* and *FFs*). For the KTU 2024 scheme, students are expected to understand the universal architectural pattern regardless of vendor branding.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a vast, complex electronic city. The FPGA itself is the city, and **Logic Cells are the identical, standardized "building blocks"** out of which every house, shop, factory, and traffic light in that city is constructed.

To make this analogy even sharper, think of a single logic cell as a **Swiss Army Knife for boolean logic**:
- The **Look-Up Table (LUT)** is like a **truth-table cheat sheet** written into a tiny ROM. You tell it which row of the cheat sheet to read, and it instantly hands you the answer (0 or 1). If you need a new logic function, you simply re-write the cheat sheet.
- The **Flip-Flop** is the **memory latch** that captures whatever the cheat sheet said one clock tick ago, so that state can be preserved across time.
- The **Carry Chain** is a **specialized express lane** for addition and subtraction, allowing the cell to add numbers much faster than if it had to chain general-purpose LUTs together.
- The **Output Multiplexer** is the **traffic controller** that decides whether the cell's output should be the live combinational value or the latched sequential value, and which direction it should be sent.

The brilliance of the FPGA is that you have **millions of these Swiss Army Knives** tiled in a regular grid, and the routing between them is also programmable. This is what makes the FPGA "field-programmable" — you are essentially rewriting millions of tiny truth tables and switch matrices to make the silicon behave as *any* digital circuit you desire.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Every KTU Student Must Memorize:**
> - **LUT Input Count (k):** Modern FPGAs use **k = 4, 5, 6, or 7** input LUTs. The industry standard high-performance FPGA LUT is **6-input** (Xilinx 7-series, Ultrascale; Altera Stratix V onwards).
> - **Logic Cell Density:** A high-end FPGA can contain **> 10 million** logic cells (e.g., Xilinx Virtex UltraScale+ VU13P has ~3.7M logic cells / ~1.7M 6-LUTs).
> - **Critical Path Delay:** Typical LUT delay is in the range of **50 ps to 200 ps** (technology node dependent: 28nm, 20nm, 14nm, 7nm).
> - **Static Power:** Modern logic cells draw near-zero DC current when not switching, but dynamic power is the dominant factor.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Architecture of a Generic Logic Cell

A canonical logic cell (as found in the Altera Cyclone series, the most common device family referenced in KTU VLSI/FPGA labs) is composed of four tightly coupled sub-blocks. We analyze each in the order data flows through them.

#### 2.1.1 The Look-Up Table (LUT) — The Combinational Brain

A **k-input LUT** is essentially a tiny SRAM of size $2^k$ entries, each entry being **1 bit** wide. The k input signals act as the address bus of this SRAM. The output of the SRAM is the result of the boolean function.

- **Why a LUT?** Any boolean function of k variables can be fully specified by its $2^k$-row truth table. By storing the truth table in SRAM, the LUT can realize *any* k-input boolean function in a single, constant-delay lookup.
- **Operation:** When the k inputs change, the address lines settle, the SRAM cell at that address is accessed, and the stored bit (0 or 1) is driven onto the LUT output. This is called the **LUT delay**, $T_{LUT}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **LUT Size vs. Logic Density Trade-off:** A 6-input LUT can implement any 6-input function in one cell, but for smaller functions (e.g., 4-input) the other $2^6 - 2^4 = 48$ SRAM bits are wasted. To recover this, modern FPGAs allow a 6-LUT to be **fractured** into two smaller 5-LUTs (or one 5-LUT + one 3-LUT, etc.). This is the core concept behind Altera's **Adaptive Logic Module (ALM)** and Xilinx's **LUT-6 fracturable** logic.

#### 2.1.2 The Programmable Flip-Flop (DFF) — The Sequential Memory

Adjacent to the LUT is a **positive-edge-triggered D-type Flip-Flop** (sometimes configurable as a latch, T-flipflop, or JK-flipflop in higher-end devices).

- **Function:** On every rising edge of the clock, the DFF samples its D input (which is the LUT output by default) and latches it onto its Q output until the next clock edge.
- **Why needed?** Combinational logic alone cannot store state. Without FFs, the FPGA could only build stateless circuits (like adders without registers). Sequential elements enable the design of state machines, counters, pipelines, and CPU registers.

#### 2.1.3 The Carry Chain — The Arithmetic Express Lane

A logic cell is incomplete without dedicated hardware for binary arithmetic. The carry chain consists of:
- A **1-bit Full Adder** (with inputs A, B, CIN).
- A **Carry Select / Carry Generate MUX** that allows the carry-out to either be the adder's carry or a programmable value.

This chain is **bypassed for general logic** and is **activated only for arithmetic primitives** (adders, subtractors, comparators, counters).

- **Why a dedicated chain?** A 32-bit ripple-carry adder built purely from LUTs would suffer $32 \times T_{LUT}$ delay. With a dedicated carry chain, the delay is reduced to roughly $32 \times T_{CARRY}$, where $T_{CARRY} \ll T_{LUT}$. The carry path propagates through a dedicated, optimized, low-skew wire that runs vertically through the logic cell column.

#### 2.1.4 The Output Multiplexer & Local Routing

A small **2:1 or 4:1 multiplexer** sits at the cell's output. It performs two jobs:
1. **Bypass vs. Register Select:** Routes either the LUT output (combinational path) or the DFF output (registered path) to the logic cell's final output pin.
2. **Feedback Routing:** Allows the LUT output to be fed back as an input to itself, which is critical for implementing wide functions that require LUT cascading (LUT expansion).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations, identities, and metrics are the most frequently asked in KTU 2024 ESE and internal examinations. Memorize the table completely.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{SRAM bits per LUT}} &= 2^{k} \\
A_{\text{logic cell area}} &\approx k \cdot 2^{k} \cdot A_{\text{SRAM6T}} + A_{\text{FF}} + A_{\text{MUX}} + A_{\text{CARRY}} \\
T_{\text{propagation, combinational}} &\approx T_{LUT} + T_{MUX} \\
T_{\text{propagation, sequential}} &\approx T_{LUT} + T_{MUX} + T_{SU} \\
f_{\text{max, register-to-register}} &= \frac{1}{T_{CO} + T_{LUT} + T_{MUX} + T_{SU}} \\
N_{\text{logic functions representable by k-LUT}} &= 2^{2^{k}} \\
\text{LUT utilization efficiency} \, \eta &= \frac{N_{\text{used LUT entries}}}{2^{k}} \times 100\% \\
\text{Carry chain delay (n-bit adder)} &\approx n \cdot T_{CARRY}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Parameter Table (No Vertical Pipes — Safe for Markdown):**

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| LUT input count | $k$ | 4, 5, 6, 7 | Defines the width of combinational functions per cell |
| SRAM bits per LUT | $2^{k}$ | 16, 32, 64, 128 | Memory cost to implement the LUT |
| Number of distinct functions | $2^{2^{k}}$ | 65,536 for $k=4$ | Combinatorial design space per LUT |
| LUT propagation delay | $T_{LUT}$ | 50–200 ps | Critical path component |
| Flip-flop setup time | $T_{SU}$ | 20–100 ps | Sequential timing constraint |
| Clock-to-output delay | $T_{CO}$ | 30–150 ps | Sequential timing parameter |
| Carry propagation delay | $T_{CARRY}$ | 10–50 ps | Arithmetic speed metric |
| Power per switching event | $P_{dyn}$ | 10–100 µW/cell/MHz | Dominant dynamic power factor |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The logic cell is the foundational unit of every digital system-on-chip designed using FPGAs. In the real engineering world, logic cells are used to implement:
- **CPU cores** (soft processors like Nios II, MicroBlaze, RISC-V) — millions of logic cells tile to form the ALU, register file, and control unit.
- **DSP pipelines** — FIR filters, FFTs, convolutions mapped directly onto logic cell arrays.
- **Communication protocol engines** — PCIe, Ethernet MAC, UART, SPI controllers.
- **Aerospace & defense control systems** — radiation-hardened FPGAs (Microsemi/ Microchip ProASIC, Xilinx Virtex-Q) use hardened logic cells for fault-tolerant design.
- **AI/ML inference accelerators** — modern FPGAs pair logic cells with tensor blocks, but small NNs and quantization logic still map efficiently onto pure logic cells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Combinational Function Representation in a k-LUT

We derive the fact that a k-input LUT can implement **any** boolean function of k variables.

**Starting Premise:** A boolean function of $k$ variables is completely and uniquely defined by its truth table, which is a mapping from each of the $2^k$ possible input combinations to a single output bit (0 or 1).

**Step 1 — Enumerate the input space:** The number of distinct input combinations is computed as the cardinality of the k-dimensional Boolean hypercube, which equals $2^k$.

$$
\text{Number of input patterns} = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times \cdots \times 2}_{k \text{ times}} = 2^{k}
$$

**Step 2 — Enumerate the output space:** For *each* input pattern, the output is independently either 0 or 1. Thus, the number of possible truth tables (i.e., distinct boolean functions) is:

$$
N_{\text{functions}} = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times \cdots \times 2}_{2^{k} \text{ times}} = 2^{2^{k}}
$$

**Step 3 — Map to physical hardware:** A k-input LUT is a $2^k \times 1$ SRAM. The k input pins serve as the address bus. For any desired boolean function, we simply load the truth table bits into the $2^k$ SRAM cells during FPGA configuration. After configuration, the LUT emulates the desired function with one access delay.

**Step 4 — Concrete numerical example for $k = 4$:** For a 4-input LUT, we have:

$$
N_{\text{functions}} = 2^{2^{4}} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536
$$

This means a single 4-LUT can implement *any one* of 65,536 different boolean functions of 4 variables — covering all 4-input logic gates, multiplexers, decoders, parity checkers, and so on.

**Step 5 — Scaling argument:** For $k = 6$ (modern high-end FPGAs):

$$
N_{\text{functions}} = 2^{2^{6}} = 2^{64} \approx 1.844 \times 10^{19}
$$

This astronomical number is why modern FPGAs can absorb incredibly complex combinational logic.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Timing Analysis of a Register-to-Register Path

Consider a sequential circuit where the output of a DFF in logic cell A feeds into a LUT in logic cell B, and the LUT output is registered by a DFF in cell B. We derive the maximum operating frequency.

**Step 1 — Identify the timing components in one clock period:** In a synchronous design, between two consecutive rising clock edges, the data must travel from the source DFF, through the routing fabric, into the destination LUT, through the destination LUT, through the output MUX, and arrive at the destination DFF *before* the next clock edge.

**Step 2 — Express the timing inequality:** For correct operation, we require:

$$
T_{CO} + T_{routing} + T_{LUT} + T_{MUX} \leq T_{CLK} - T_{SU}
$$

Rearranging for the clock period $T_{CLK}$:

$$
T_{CLK} \geq T_{CO} + T_{routing} + T_{LUT} + T_{MUX} + T_{SU}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the minimum clock period and maximum frequency:**

$$
T_{CLK, \min} = T_{CO} + T_{routing} + T_{LUT} + T_{MUX} + T_{SU}
$$

$$
f_{\max} = \frac{1}{T_{CLK, \min}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical worked example:** Suppose the vendor datasheet gives the following values for a 28nm FPGA:

$$
T_{CO} = 80 \, \text{ps}, \quad T_{routing} = 150 \, \text{ps}, \quad T_{LUT} = 100 \, \text{ps}, \quad T_{MUX} = 40 \, \text{ps}, \quad T_{SU} = 60 \, \text{ps}
$$

Summing all terms:

$$
T_{CLK, \min} = 80 + 150 + 100 + 40 + 60 = 430 \, \text{ps}
$$

Converting to frequency:

$$
f_{\max} = \frac{1}{430 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 2.326 \, \text{GHz}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:** This 2.3 GHz figure is the *theoretical* maximum for this particular register-to-register path. Real designs typically achieve 60–80% of this value due to routing congestion, logic depth variations, and clock skew.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Vendor-Specific Logic Cell Implementations

The following matrix contrasts how the three major FPGA vendors (Xilinx, Intel/Altera, Lattice) physically implement the abstract "Logic Cell" concept. This is a high-yield comparison for KTU viva and short-answer questions.

| Vendor | Branding | Atomic Unit | LUT Size | Notable Enhancement | Example Device |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Xilinx | Slice (CLB) | 6-LUT + 2 DFFs + Carry | 6-input | LUT fracturable into two 5-LUTs | Artix-7, Kintex-7 |
| Intel (Altera) | Logic Element (LE) | 4-LUT + DFF + Carry | 4-input (Cyclone) | Adaptive Logic Module (ALM) is 8-input fracturable | Cyclone IV, Cyclone V |
| Lattice Semiconductor | Slice / PFU | 4-LUT + DFF + Carry | 4-input | Wide LUT in ECP5 series (8 fracturable LUTs per PFU) | ECP5, iCE40 |
| Microchip (Microsemi) | Logic Tile | 4-LUT + DFF + Carry | 4-input | Radiation-hardened silicon-on-sapphire | ProASIC3, RTG4 |

**Step-by-step logic of the comparison:**
1. **Xilinx's choice of 6-input LUTs** strikes a balance — large enough to absorb complex boolean functions (like a 6-input decoder in a single LUT), yet small enough to keep SRAM bit count manageable ($2^6 = 64$ bits per LUT).
2. **Altera's 4-input LUTs in Cyclone** were a legacy design choice kept for cost reasons. To compensate for the smaller function size, Altera introduced the **ALM** in Stratix, which is an 8-input fracturable LUT that can be split into multiple smaller LUTs as the design requires.
3. **Lattice focuses on low-power, low-cost** FPGAs, hence the 4-input LUT and the **Programmable Functional Unit (PFU)** organization.
4. **Microchip specializes in aerospace and defense**, where reliability trumps density. Their logic cells are simpler but built on radiation-tolerant processes.

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Area Cost of a Logic Cell

The total silicon area of a logic cell determines how many cells can be fabricated per unit die area, which in turn dictates FPGA cost and capacity.

**Step 1 — Decompose area into functional blocks:**

$$
A_{\text{cell}} = A_{\text{LUT}} + A_{\text{FF}} + A_{\text{MUX}} + A_{\text{CARRY}} + A_{\text{ROUTE}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known area models:** A 6T-SRAM bit cell occupies an area $A_{\text{SRAM6T}}$ (typically $\approx 0.5 \, \mu\text{m}^2$ at 28nm). A DFF takes roughly $12$ to $15$ transistors, which is approximately $2 \times A_{\text{SRAM6T}}$.

$$
A_{\text{LUT}} = 2^{k} \cdot A_{\text{SRAM6T}}
$$

$$
A_{\text{FF}} \approx 2 \cdot A_{\text{SRAM6T}}
$$

$$
A_{\text{MUX}} \approx 3 \cdot A_{\text{SRAM6T}}
$$

$$
A_{\text{CARRY}} \approx 2 \cdot A_{\text{SRAM6T}}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine for $k = 6$:**

$$
A_{\text{cell}} \approx 64 \cdot A_{\text{SRAM6T}} + 2 \cdot A_{\text{SRAM6T}} + 3 \cdot A_{\text{SRAM6T}} + 2 \cdot A_{\text{SRAM6T}} = 71 \cdot A_{\text{SRAM6T}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation at 28nm ($A_{\text{SRAM6T}} = 0.5 \, \mu\text{m}^2$):**

$$
A_{\text{cell}} \approx 71 \times 0.5 = 35.5 \, \mu\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Density interpretation:** On a $400 \, \text{mm}^2$ die, the theoretical maximum number of logic cells is:

$$
N_{\text{max cells}} = \frac{400 \times 10^{6} \, \mu\text{m}^2}{35.5 \, \mu\text{m}^2} \approx 11.3 \text{ million cells}
$$

This is why modern flagship FPGAs can contain tens of millions of logic cells while still fitting in a reasonable package.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic FPGA Logic Cell

The following Mermaid diagram shows the complete internal architecture of a typical logic cell, illustrating the four primary sub-blocks and the data flow between them. The architecture shown corresponds to the Altera Cyclone-series LE and is general enough to apply (with minor relabeling) to any vendor.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef inputStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef lutStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef ffStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef carryStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef muxStyle fill:#e2d5e6,stroke:#5b2c6f,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000

    InA["Input A"]:::inputStyle
    InB["Input B"]:::inputStyle
    InC["Input C"]:::inputStyle
    InD["Input D"]:::inputStyle
    CIN["Carry-In CIN"]:::inputStyle

    LUTblock["4-input or 6-input Look-Up Table SRAM 2^k entries"]:::lutStyle
    FAblock["1-bit Full Adder Sum and Carry Logic"]:::carryStyle
    Cout["Carry-Out COUT"]:::outputStyle

    DFFblock["D-type Flip-Flop Edge-Triggered"]:::ffStyle
    MUXout["Output Multiplexer Registered or Bypass Select"]:::muxStyle
    OutLE["Logic Cell Output to Routing Fabric"]:::outputStyle

    Feedback["Local Feedback Path to LUT input"]:::outputStyle

    InA --> LUTblock
    InB --> LUTblock
    InC --> LUTblock
    InD --> LUTblock
    InA --> FAblock
    InB --> FAblock
    CIN --> FAblock
    FAblock --> Cout

    LUTblock --> DFFblock
    LUTblock --> MUXout
    DFFblock --> MUXout
    LUTblock --> Feedback
    Feedback --> LUTblock
    MUXout --> OutLE
```

### 4.2 Tiled Logic Cell Array — FPGA Logic Fabric

The following diagram shows how individual logic cells are arranged in a 2D grid surrounded by programmable interconnect. This is the top-level view of the FPGA logic fabric.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef cellStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef swStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef ioStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000

    LE1["Logic Cell 1,0"]:::cellStyle
    LE2["Logic Cell 1,1"]:::cellStyle
    LE3["Logic Cell 1,2"]:::cellStyle
    LE4["Logic Cell 2,0"]:::cellStyle
    LE5["Logic Cell 2,1"]:::cellStyle
    LE6["Logic Cell 2,2"]:::cellStyle
    LE7["Logic Cell 3,0"]:::cellStyle
    LE8["Logic Cell 3,1"]:::cellStyle
    LE9["Logic Cell 3,2"]:::cellStyle

    SwitchA["Switch Matrix H-track and V-track"]:::swStyle
    SwitchB["Switch Matrix H-track and V-track"]:::swStyle
    SwitchC["Switch Matrix H-track and V-track"]:::swStyle
    SwitchD["Switch Matrix H-track and V-track"]:::swStyle

    IOB["IO Block Pin Driver and Receiver"]:::ioStyle

    LE1 --- SwitchA
    LE2 --- SwitchA
    LE4 --- SwitchA
    LE5 --- SwitchA
    LE2 --- SwitchB
    LE3 --- SwitchB
    LE5 --- SwitchB
    LE6 --- SwitchB
    LE4 --- SwitchC
    LE5 --- SwitchC
    LE7 --- SwitchC
    LE8 --- SwitchC
    LE5 --- SwitchD
    LE6 --- SwitchD
    LE8 --- SwitchD
    LE9 --- SwitchD

    SwitchA --- IOB
    SwitchB --- IOB
    SwitchC --- IOB
    SwitchD --- IOB
```

### 4.3 Data Path State Machine — LUT Loading and Operation

This Mermaid sequence diagram clarifies the two-phase operation of a logic cell: **Configuration Phase** (during FPGA power-up, when the LUT is loaded) and **Operation Phase** (during normal user logic execution).

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PowerUp
    PowerUp --> ConfigLoad: FPGA bitstream arrives
    ConfigLoad --> LUTProgrammed: SRAM cells written with truth table
    LUTProgrammed --> UserOperation: Device enters user mode
    UserOperation --> InputsArrive: k input signals settle
    InputsArrive --> LUTLookup: Address lines drive SRAM row
    LUTLookup --> LUTOutput: SRAM bit driven to output
    LUTOutput --> BypassPath: Direct to output MUX
    LUTOutput --> DFFsample: Captured on next clock edge
    DFFsample --> DFFhold: Output stable until next clock
    BypassPath --> [*]
    DFFhold --> InputsArrive: Next computation cycle
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**  
*Define an FPGA Logic Cell. List its four main sub-blocks and state the function of each in one sentence.*

**Model Answer:**

An **FPGA Logic Cell** is the smallest reconfigurable logic unit in an FPGA that implements a combinational or sequential boolean function. Its four main sub-blocks are:

1. **Look-Up Table (LUT):** Implements any k-input combinational function by storing its truth table in a $2^k \times 1$ SRAM.
2. **Flip-Flop (DFF):** Provides sequential state storage by latching the LUT output on a clock edge.
3. **Carry Chain (Full Adder + Carry MUX):** Performs fast 1-bit arithmetic and propagates carry signals between adjacent cells for high-speed adders.
4. **Output Multiplexer:** Selects between the combinational (LUT) and registered (DFF) outputs and routes them to the global routing fabric.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**  
*What is the difference between a 4-input LUT and a 6-input LUT in terms of (a) SRAM size, (b) number of implementable functions, and (c) silicon area? Justify why modern high-end FPGAs prefer 6-input LUTs.*

**Model Answer:**

| Metric | 4-input LUT | 6-input LUT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| SRAM size | $2^{4} = 16$ bits | $2^{6} = 64$ bits |
| Implementable functions | $2^{2^{4}} = 65{,}536$ | $2^{2^{6}} = 2^{64}$ |
| Silicon area (relative) | $1 \times$ (baseline) | $4 \times$ (4× the bits) |

Modern high-end FPGAs prefer 6-input LUTs because:
- A single 6-LUT can absorb a wider combinational cone, **reducing logic depth** and **improving $f_{\max}$**.
- Although 6-LUTs use 4× the SRAM, the **routing area saved** (fewer inter-cell nets) more than compensates.
- 6-LUTs can be **fractured** into smaller LUTs when the design needs them, preserving utilization efficiency.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Main Slot]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the internal architecture of a generic FPGA logic cell. Identify each sub-block and describe its role in detail. Briefly discuss how the LUT implements any arbitrary boolean function of k variables.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 32-bit ripple-carry adder is to be implemented on an FPGA. Compare the design using only LUTs versus using the dedicated carry chain. Derive the timing equations for both cases and show that the carry chain offers an order-of-magnitude speedup. Use the following typical values: $T_{LUT} = 100$ ps, $T_{CARRY} = 30$ ps, $T_{MUX} = 40$ ps, $T_{SU} = 60$ ps.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The internal architecture consists of four blocks (refer to the diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Look-Up Table (LUT):** A $2^k \times 1$ SRAM. The k inputs serve as the address bus. To implement an arbitrary boolean function of k variables, we observe that a boolean function is uniquely defined by its truth table of $2^k$ rows. Since each row's output is an independent 1-bit choice (0 or 1), there are $2^{2^k}$ possible functions. By loading any one of these truth tables into the LUT's SRAM during configuration, the LUT can realize that function during operation. The LUT output is purely combinational.

2. **Flip-Flop (DFF):** A positive-edge-triggered D-type flip-flop captures the LUT output on the rising clock edge and holds it stable on the Q output. This enables sequential logic and pipelining. The DFF is also configurable as a latch or toggle flip-flop in higher-end devices.

3. **Carry Chain:** A dedicated 1-bit full adder with inputs A, B, and CIN, plus a carry-out MUX for carry-skip optimization. The carry-out is routed directly to the CIN of the cell below, bypassing the slow general routing. This chain is activated only for arithmetic primitives.

4. **Output Multiplexer and Feedback:** Selects registered (DFF Q) or combinational (LUT output) as the cell's final output, and provides local feedback for LUT expansion (cascading multiple LUTs to implement wider functions).

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Case 1 — Pure LUT Implementation of 32-bit Adder:**

In a pure-LUT adder, the carry must propagate from bit 0 to bit 31, but each bit's sum is computed by a LUT that takes A, B, and CIN as inputs. Since CIN arrives from the previous bit's LUT output (through general routing), the carry path delay per bit is $T_{LUT} + T_{routing}$. For 32 bits:

$$
T_{\text{adder, LUT}} = 32 \times (T_{LUT} + T_{routing}) + T_{MUX} + T_{SU}
$$

Assuming $T_{routing} \approx T_{LUT} = 100$ ps:

$$
T_{\text{adder, LUT}} = 32 \times 200 + 40 + 60 = 6500 \, \text{ps} = 6.5 \, \text{ns}
$$

The maximum adder frequency is:

$$
f_{\text{max, LUT}} = \frac{1}{6.5 \, \text{ns}} \approx 153.8 \, \text{MHz}
$$

**Case 2 — Dedicated Carry Chain Implementation:**

With the carry chain, the carry-in to bit $i+1$ arrives via the dedicated, fast, hardwired carry line, not through general routing. The delay per bit is just $T_{CARRY}$:

$$
T_{\text{adder, CARRY}} = 32 \times T_{CARRY} + T_{MUX} + T_{SU}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{\text{adder, CARRY}} = 32 \times 30 + 40 + 60 = 1060 \, \text{ps} \approx 1.06 \, \text{ns}
$$

The maximum adder frequency is:

$$
f_{\text{max, CARRY}} = \frac{1}{1.06 \, \text{ns}} \approx 943.4 \, \text{MHz}
$$

**Conclusion — Speedup Comparison:**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{\text{adder, LUT}}}{T_{\text{adder, CARRY}}} = \frac{6.5}{1.06} \approx 6.13 \times
$$

The dedicated carry chain provides approximately a **6× speedup** for a 32-bit adder. This justifies why every modern FPGA integrates a dedicated carry chain into its logic cell — without it, arithmetic-intensive designs (DSP, AI inference) would be impractical.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Alternative Slot]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a 6-input Look-Up Table (6-LUT). Show how a 6-LUT can be fractured into two 5-LUTs. Why is LUT fracturing a critical feature in modern FPGAs?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the logic cell architectures of Xilinx, Altera (Intel), and Lattice in a tabular form. Discuss how the choice of LUT size impacts the trade-off between logic density, performance, and routing congestion.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**6-LUT Architecture:** A 6-input LUT is a $2^6 \times 1 = 64 \times 1$ SRAM array. The 6 input signals ($I_0$ through $I_5$) drive a 6-to-64 decoder. Each of the 64 decoder outputs enables one row of the SRAM. The selected SRAM cell's content (a single bit, pre-loaded during FPGA configuration) is driven onto the LUT output line.

```
LUT6:  Inputs I0..I5 → 6:64 Decoder → 64x1 SRAM → 1-bit Output
```

**LUT Fracturing — Splitting into Two 5-LUTs:**

A 6-LUT can be conceptually divided by fixing one of its 6 inputs (say $I_5$) to two different constant values:

- When $I_5 = 0$: A 5-input LUT (using $I_0$ through $I_4$) is realized, occupying the first 32 SRAM rows.
- When $I_5 = 1$: Another independent 5-input LUT (using $I_0$ through $I_4$) is realized, occupying the last 32 SRAM rows.

The output is then selected by a 2:1 MUX controlled by $I_5$:

$$
\text{LUT6\_out} = \overline{I_5} \cdot \text{LUT5\_A}(I_0, I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4) + I_5 \cdot \text{LUT5\_B}(I_0, I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4)
$$

**Why LUT Fracturing is Critical:**

1. **Utilization Recovery:** A user design might have a 5-input function and a separate 3-input function that would *both* fit inside one 6-LUT if fractured. Without fracturing, the 6-LUT would be wasted on the 5-input function alone.
2. **Logic Density:** A 6-LUT can effectively become two 5-LUTs (or one 5-LUT + one 3-LUT, etc.), nearly doubling the effective logic capacity.
3. **Performance Optimization:** Smaller LUTs have shorter internal critical paths, allowing $f_{\max}$ to be higher for narrow but deep logic paths.
4. **Power Reduction:** A fractured LUT typically switches less internal circuitry, lowering dynamic power.

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Vendor | Branding | Atomic Unit | LUT Size | Unique Feature | Example Device |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Xilinx | Slice (CLB) | 6-LUT + 2 DFFs | 6-input | LUT fracturable; carry chain per slice | Artix-7, Virtex-7 |
| Intel (Altera) | Logic Element (LE) | 4-LUT + DFF (Cyclone) | 4-input (legacy) | ALM in Stratix is 8-input fracturable | Cyclone IV, Stratix V |
| Lattice | Slice / PFU | 4-LUT + DFF | 4-input | Low-power, low-cost focus | ECP5, iCE40 |

**Trade-off Analysis:**

- **Smaller LUTs (k=4):** Higher logic density per unit area (less SRAM per cell), but deeper logic for complex functions → more routing congestion, lower $f_{\max}$.
- **Larger LUTs (k=6):** Lower logic density per unit area (more SRAM per cell), but shallower logic for complex functions → less routing congestion, higher $f_{\max}$.
- **Fracturable LUTs:** Best of both worlds. Modern Xilinx and Altera devices use 6-input (or 8-input) fracturable LUTs to balance density and performance adaptively based on the actual design.

The industry has converged on **6-input fracturable LUTs** as the sweet spot for general-purpose FPGAs, which is why the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes this architecture.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Logic Cell" with "LUT":** A logic cell is *not* just a LUT — it includes the FF, carry chain, and MUX. Students who define "logic cell = LUT" alone will lose 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the Carry Chain:** Many students draw only LUT + FF and miss the dedicated carry logic. Always mention the carry chain in the diagram and timing analysis.
> 3. **Timing Derivation Errors:** When deriving $f_{\max}$, students often forget $T_{SU}$ or $T_{CO}$. The complete inequality is $T_{CLK} \geq T_{CO} + T_{routing} + T_{LUT} + T_{MUX} + T_{SU}$.
> 4. **Numerical Units:** Mixing ps and ns in the same calculation will be penalized. Standardize units before summing.
> 5. **LUT Fracturing Confusion:** Students often think a 6-LUT becomes "two 3-LUTs" or "three 2-LUTs." The correct fracture is into two 5-LUTs sharing 5 inputs (with the 6th input used as a MUX select).
> 6. **Vendor Terminology Mix-up:** Do not use "LE" and "Slice" interchangeably without clarifying which vendor you are referring to.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam Preparation:**

- **Logic Cell Definition:** Smallest reconfigurable unit = LUT + FF + Carry Chain + Output MUX.
- **LUT Principle:** A k-input LUT is a $2^k \times 1$ SRAM; can implement any of $2^{2^k}$ boolean functions.
- **Standard k-values:** 4 (Cyclone), 5, 6 (modern Xilinx/Altera), 7 (some high-end).
- **LUT Fracturing:** A 6-LUT can split into 2 independent 5-LUTs; critical for utilization.
- **Flip-Flop Role:** Enables sequential logic; sampled on rising clock edge.
- **Carry Chain:** Dedicated fast adder chain for arithmetic; ~6× speedup over LUT-only adder.
- **Output MUX:** Selects registered vs. combinational path.
- **Timing Equation:** $f_{\max} = 1 / (T_{CO} + T_{routing} + T_{LUT} + T_{MUX} + T_{SU})$.
- **Vendor Mapping:** Xilinx → Slice (6-LUT); Altera → LE / ALM; Lattice → PFU.
- **Common Exam Keywords:** "Look-Up Table," "Fracturable LUT," "Carry Chain," "Logic Element," "Slice," "CLB," "Adaptive Logic Module," "Sequential Element," "Combinational Path," "Critical Path Delay."
- **High-Yield Numbers:** $2^4 = 16$ bits, $2^6 = 64$ bits, $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ functions, $2^{64}$ functions for 6-LUT.
- **Typical Delay Values to Memorize:** $T_{LUT} \approx 100$ ps, $T_{CARRY} \approx 30$ ps, $T_{SU} \approx 60$ ps, $T_{CO} \approx 80$ ps.
- **Industrial Relevance:** Logic cells are the building blocks of soft-core CPUs, DSP pipelines, communication engines, and AI accelerators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interconnection Network and I/O Pad
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT502 - FPGA BASED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interconnection Network and I/O Pad

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Interconnection Network in FPGA

The **Interconnection Network** (also called the **Routing Architecture** or **Programmable Routing Network**) in an FPGA is the programmable fabric of wires, programmable switches, multiplexers, and switch boxes that connect thousands of Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs), Block RAMs (BRAMs), DSP slices, and I/O pads together to implement a user-defined digital circuit.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme, the interconnection network is the dominant consumer of silicon area, delay, and power in any modern FPGA. The CLB contributes only about **20–30%** of the total chip area, while the routing network occupies roughly **60–70%** of the silicon real estate. The remaining area is consumed by I/O pads and hard-IP blocks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEVT502 – Module 2):**
> The interconnection network provides three primary classes of routing resources: **local routing**, **general-purpose routing**, and **global/dedicated routing (long lines)**. Each segment is broken at switch matrices (SMs) and connected through programmable interconnect points (PIPs) and connection boxes (CBs).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway System of an FPGA"

Think of an FPGA as a **giant city**:
- The **CLBs** are the houses and offices where all the work happens.
- The **I/O pads** are the city gates connecting to the outside world.
- The **interconnection network** is the network of roads, flyovers, and traffic signals.

When you program an FPGA, you are essentially the **city planner** drawing roadmaps between houses so that signals (cars) can travel from one logic block to another with minimum traffic (delay) and minimum fuel consumption (power). Short roads handle local traffic (single-length lines), main avenues handle medium traffic (double/hex lines), and expressways (long lines / global clocks) carry high-speed signals across the entire city.

## 1.2 I/O Pads in FPGA

The **I/O Pad** (Input/Output Pad) is a specialized, hardened physical interface cell located at the periphery of the FPGA die. Each I/O pad acts as the boundary controller between the internal core logic (operating at a low core voltage, typically 1.0 V or 1.2 V) and the external package pins (which must communicate with chips operating at various standards like 3.3 V, 2.5 V, 1.8 V, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (I/O Pad):**
> An I/O pad is a configurable buffer/circuit comprising an **input path**, an **output path** (with optional tri-state control), an **I/O register** (for SDR and DDR operations), **ESD protection diodes**, **pull-up/pull-down resistors**, and **slew rate / drive strength control**, all surrounding a single package pin.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Customs and Immigration Counter of the FPGA"

Imagine the FPGA die as a country and the outside PCB as foreign soil. Every signal entering or leaving the FPGA must pass through the **I/O pad**, which behaves like a **customs & immigration counter**:
- It **translates voltage levels** (the country speaks 1.0 V internally, but visitors speak 3.3 V, 1.8 V, etc.).
- It **checks the validity** of incoming signals (input synchronization, Schmitt trigger).
- It **stamps and authenticates outgoing signals** (slew-rate control, drive strength).
- It **protects the country** from electrical threats (ESD protection, clamping diodes).

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Core Voltage (V<sub>CCINT</sub>) | **0.9 V – 1.2 V** | Powers internal logic & routing |
| I/O Voltage (V<sub>CCO</sub>) | **1.2 V – 3.3 V** | Powers I/O pad output drivers |
| Routing Resistance per segment | **50 Ω – 500 Ω** | Per wire segment |
| Routing Capacitance per segment | **10 fF – 50 fF** | Per wire segment, per PIP |
| ESD Human-Body Model (HBM) Rating | **±2 kV to ±15 kV** | I/O pad protection target |
| Drive Strength (LVCMOS) | **2 mA – 24 mA** | Output current capability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (KTU 2024 Board Favorite):**
> - **Interconnection Network** is INSIDE the FPGA, between CLBs.
> - **I/O Pad** is ON THE BOUNDARY, between the FPGA core and the package pin.
> These are sometimes confused in the exam. Always specify "internal routing" vs. "external interface" in your answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the FPGA Interconnection Network

The routing fabric is built from a hierarchical stack of resources:

### 2.1.1 Wire Segments (Lengths)

Modern FPGAs offer **four classes of wire segments**, each trading off speed, area, and flexibility:

1. **Single-Length Lines (Local Routing)** — span only **one CLB**. They connect to switch matrices on every block boundary. Excellent for short, dense, local connections but slow across long distances.
2. **Double-Length Lines (Medium Routing)** — span **two CLBs**. Provide faster intermediate-distance routing.
3. **Quad/Long Lines (Hex Lines)** — span **four or six CLBs**. Useful for medium-fanout nets.
4. **Global Long Lines (High-Speed Routing)** — span the **entire chip** width. Used for **clock distribution, reset, and high-fanout enable nets**. Often driven by dedicated global clock buffers (BUFG in Xilinx, GLB in Intel).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this hierarchy?** Short wires are abundant (great for local nets, low capacitance per hop). Long wires are scarce (reserved for critical signals). The mix is statistically optimized for the Rent's-rule distribution of typical net lengths.

### 2.1.2 Programmable Interconnect Points (PIPs)

A **PIP** is the atomic switch element in the routing. It is realized by one of three technologies:

- **SRAM-controlled pass transistor** (used in Xilinx 7-series, UltraScale; Intel Cyclone/Stratix).
- **Antifuse** (used in Actel/Microsemi/Microchip antifuse FPGAs — one-time programmable).
- **Flash-controlled floating gate** (used in Microsemi IGLOO, ProASIC3).

The pass transistor is a single NMOS (or transmission gate) gated by a 6T/8T SRAM cell, turning a horizontal/vertical wire connection ON or OFF.

### 2.1.3 Switch Matrix (SM) and Connection Box (CB)

- **Connection Box (CB)**: where an **input pin of a CLB** connects to the surrounding wires (fan-in point). Configurable subset of wires connect to each pin.
- **Switch Box (SB/SM)**: where **horizontal and vertical wires cross**; configurable subset of connections between them (Wilton, Universal, Disjoint topologies).

### 2.1.4 Routing Topology Variants

| Topology | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Symmetric / Disjoint** | Each wire enters only one SB per tile | Minimum area, lowest flexibility |
| **Wilton Switch Box** | Each pin has Fs=3 connections, flexible | Used in VPR (academic tool) |
| **Universal Switch Box** | All 4 directions interconnected via Fs=3 | Maximum routability, higher area |
| **Imran / Indirect** | Multiplexer-based (used in Stratix, Cyclone) | High density, fast |

## 2.2 I/O Pad Architecture — Block-Level Details

A modern FPGA I/O pad (e.g., Xilinx SelectIO) consists of:

### 2.2.1 Input Path
- **Differential pair buffer** (for LVDS, HSTL, SSTL).
- **Single-ended input buffer** with **Schmitt trigger** option (for noisy LVTTL inputs).
- **Input register / Input DDR (IDDR)** — samples data on 1 or 2 edges of an I/O clock.
- **Programmable delay / input serializer/deserializer (ISERDES)** — for high-speed source-synchronous interfaces.

### 2.2.2 Output Path
- **Output register / Output DDR (ODDR)** — generates data on 1 or 2 edges.
- **Tri-state control** (T pin) — for bidirectional buses.
- **Slew rate control** (slow / fast) — for EMI management.
- **Drive strength control** (2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 16, 24 mA for LVCMOS).
- **Open-drain / open-source** options (for I²C, wired-OR buses).

### 2.2.3 Common I/O Standards Supported

| Standard | Voltage | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **LVTTL** | 3.3 V | Legacy 5 V-tolerant TTL |
| **LVCMOS33** | 3.3 V | General-purpose 3.3 V CMOS |
| **LVCMOS18** | 1.8 V | Modern low-power |
| **LVCMOS12** | 1.2 V | High-speed chip-to-chip |
| **SSTL18 / SSTL15** | 1.8 V / 1.5 V | DDR2/DDR3 memory |
| **HSTL_I** | 1.5 V | High-speed SRAM |
| **LVDS** | ±350 mV differential | High-speed serial, >1 Gbps |
| **PCI / PCI-X** | 3.3 V / 5 V | Computer bus standard |
| **TMDS / HDMI** | 3.3 V differential | Display interfaces |

### 2.2.4 I/O Banking

FPGAs group I/O pins into **banks** (typically 4 to 16 per chip). Each bank shares a single **V<sub>CCO</sub>** (output driver supply) but a common internal **V<sub>REF</sub>** for SSTL/HSTL differential reference. A single bank must use **one I/O standard** (or carefully compatible ones).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Mixing SSTL18 and LVCMOS33 in the same bank is **NOT allowed** because V<sub>REF</sub> and V<sub>CCO</sub> requirements differ. Examiners frequently test this concept.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_w = R_{\square} \cdot \dfrac{L}{W}$ | Resistance of a wire segment of length L, width W, sheet resistance R□ | Ω |
| 2 | $C_w = C_{\text{area}} \cdot W \cdot L + C_{\text{fringe}} \cdot 2 \cdot (W+L)$ | Total wire capacitance (area + fringe) | F |
| 3 | $t_d \approx 0.69 \cdot R \cdot C$ | RC delay (first-order, Elmore) | s |
| 4 | $t_d^{\text{Elmore}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i \cdot C_i$ | Elmore delay for RC ladder | s |
| 5 | $\tau_{\text{rep}} = R_{dr} \cdot (C_{\text{int}} + C_{\text{load}})$ | Replicated-driver delay (buffered long line) | s |
